script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Tuesday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed four point six zero percent down on the session. Broad semiconductor exposure took a hit across the board, with no position in the top ten finishing in positive territory.

The single most important dynamic driving Tuesday's session: profit taking rotated hard out of chip names and into enterprise software. Every primary holding closed lower. The steepest drops landed in Marvell Technology, Intel, and Lam Research, none of which had obvious company-specific catalysts — making the selling look systematic rather than fundamental.

Chip stocks fell sharply as Middle East tensions escalated, sending oil prices higher and weighing on risk sentiment broadly. Reports indicate the Nasdaq composite declined meaningfully in Tuesday's session alongside semiconductor-heavy indices, with traders pulling back from high-multiple technology names ahead of a heavy earnings week. No major Federal Reserve commentary moved markets on Tuesday.

The top story from Tuesday centers on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company and what its results signal for the broader AI hardware trade. TSMC reported sales growth of thirty-six percent, a blowout quarter that underscores sustained demand for advanced logic chips used in artificial intelligence infrastructure. However, the read-through for portfolio names is nuanced. Memory stocks cratered in the same session, with Micron among the hardest hit, suggesting that while AI accelerator demand remains intact, the memory segment may be showing early signs of demand softening or supply pressure. According to reporting from finance.yahoo.com, the divergence signals something important: AI hardware demand is holding up specifically at the logic and foundry layer — where Taiwan Semiconductor sits — while commodity memory names face different headwinds. For this portfolio, that split matters. Positions concentrated in memory and equipment face a different risk profile than pure-play logic exposure right now.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point two one percent on the session. Reporting from finance.yahoo.com noted the decline was larger than the general market move, and context from the TSMC sales report suggests AMD, whose chips run through that same Taiwan foundry, remains foundry-dependent in a positive sense — demand for its AI accelerators has not been called into question. The catalyst to watch is TSMC's full earnings call on Wednesday.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down four point three two percent. The ASML earnings report due Wednesday is the critical event here. Reporting from finance.yahoo.com noted ASML is expected to address capacity constraints and the impact of U.S. export controls that would curb sales of less-advanced tools to China, which currently accounts for up to twenty percent of ASML's revenue. That has direct implications for Micron's equipment supply chain.

Nvidia closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, down three point five two percent. Tuesday's decline was more moderate than most peers, consistent with its foundry relationship at Taiwan Semiconductor holding firm. No Nvidia-specific catalyst drove the move.

Turning to Broadcom — the company closed at three hundred eighty-four dollars and five cents, down three point nine eight percent. Notably, reporting from finance.yahoo.com highlighted that Broadcom extended its custom chip agreement with Apple through twenty thirty-one, covering multiple generations of custom silicon. That long-dated contract reinforces the company's infrastructure role even on a down session.

Over at Intel, the session was notably painful. Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down six point one two percent — the second-worst performer in the portfolio on Tuesday. No specific company news drove the move, suggesting Intel's broader competitive positioning concerns and restructuring narrative weighed on sentiment as the sector sold off.

Applied Materials closed at five hundred seventy-five dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point five zero percent. As a major equipment supplier, Applied Materials sits in the crosshairs of any export control tightening narrative circulating around ASML. Investors will watch the ASML report closely for read-through.

KLA Corporation closed at two hundred twenty-two dollars and twenty-five cents, down four point zero zero percent. The equipment sector sold off in sympathy with the broader ASML and export control narrative. No company-specific news was reported Tuesday.

Meanwhile, Marvell Technology posted the worst session in the portfolio — closing at two hundred seventeen dollars and fifty-three cents, down seven point seven five percent. No direct company news surfaced, making this a technically driven or sentiment-driven move in a name that has run significantly year-to-date.

Lam Research closed at three hundred twenty-nine dollars and ninety-two cents, down five point eight three percent. Like Applied Materials and KLA Corporation, Lam is a semiconductor equipment name with exposure to any China export restriction fallout from the pending U.S. legislation.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company closed at four hundred twenty-one dollars and fifty-eight cents, down two point eight nine percent — the most resilient name in the portfolio on Tuesday, consistent with the strong sales data reported for the quarter.

Three items to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, ASML quarterly earnings on Wednesday — direct read-through for Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA Corporation on equipment demand and China exposure. Second, the full TSMC earnings call on Wednesday, which will give forward guidance on capacity, pricing, and AI chip demand. Third, watch for any congressional updates on the proposed U.S. export control alignment legislation that specifically names ASML and could reshape the equipment supply chain.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Wednesday's ASML and TSMC calls are the events that matter most — watch for guidance language on China and AI infrastructure demand.
